It can make them perform worse or avoid those fields altogether, even if they're talented. I've felt something similar in my work—sometimes clients assume older hairdressers can't keep up with new styles, and it gets me down, so I understand how damaging these ideas can be. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: As a 66-year-old hairdresser in Kansas City, I've seen a lot in my life, and I think about stereotype threat in STEM from my own experiences. Stereotype threat is when people feel pressured or worried because of negative stereotypes about their group, like women or minorities being told they're not good at math or science. It can make them perform worse or avoid those fields altogether, even if they're talented. I've felt something similar in my work—sometimes clients assume older hairdressers can't keep up with new styles, and it gets me down, so I understand how damaging these ideas can be. We should definitely address it because it's unfair and holds people back from opportunities. In my view, based on my moderate conscientiousness and high extraversion, I believe in talking openly about these issues. We need more encouragement and support in schools and workplaces, like mentors who can guide young people and show them they belong. As someone who's depressed and high in neuroticism, I know how negative thoughts can spiral, so creating positive environments is key. From my Islamic beliefs, I value fairness and helping others, so addressing stereotype threat aligns with that. We can do this by promoting diverse role models in STEM, offering training to reduce biases, and making sure everyone feels welcome. It's not just about fixing problems but building a community where everyone can thrive, much like how I try to make my salon a friendly place for all clients.
